comments: Cosmogonic emanation. Siva as masculine power produces the Mummurttis. Sakti produces Nava-Sakti (9 goddesses). Rudra, Mahesvara, Sadasiva are alternate names of Siva/Isvara, not separate beings.

Isvara identical with Siva. Parvati identified with Sakti by Saivas. Also called Uma, Devi, Gauri, Kali, Durga, Ambika, etc. The text says children are not begotten naturally but originate by divine will. Virabhadra also produced by Isvara (Part III Ch.V) but from wrath, not from Parvati.

Secondary wife. Ganga is the river Ganges personified. No children attributed to this union in the text. Represented as a siren (half woman half fish). Not worshipped with images in pagodas but honored through bathing rituals.
Devayanai (also Devasena) is the daughter of Devendra (Indra). First wife, married after Subhramanya's victory over the giant Sura. No children mentioned.
Valliammai was born of a roe and brought up by basket-makers. Second wife. No children mentioned. Devadasis (temple dancing-girls) are betrothed to Subhramanya.

Lakshmi is the goddess of beauty and fortune. Identified with Sakti by Vaishnavas. Manmatha (Cupid/Kama) is the 'mental son' of Vishnu. When Vishnu incarnated as Rama, Lakshmi was Sita, and their sons were Kusa and Lava (listed under Rama-Sita couple). Lakshmi rose from sea of milk. Eight forms (Ashta-Lakshmi).
Bhumidevi is the goddess of the earth. Secondary wife. Not worshipped; no image in pagodas. No children attributed to this union.

Manmatha (Kama/Cupid) is the god of sensual love, mental son of Vishnu. Rati (Lust) is his wife. Burnt to ashes by Isvara's third eye for disturbing his meditation, then revived but made invisible to all except Rati. No children listed in this text. (Other sources give Pradyumna as incarnation of Manmatha, with son Aniruddha.)

Rama is Vishnu's seventh incarnation, son of Dasaratha. Sita is Lakshmi incarnate. Kusa born to Sita; Lava said to be created by Rishi Valmiki from Darbha grass and adopted by Sita/Rama. Text describes Rama's repudiation and later reconciliation with Sita.

Krishna is Vishnu's eighth incarnation. Rukmini was his only lawful wife. Pradyumna identified as incarnation of Manmatha. Text says Krishna had 16000 mistresses including seven favorites, plus Radha. Text claims 108000 sons total but names only Pradyumna.

Sarasvati is goddess of learning. No children explicitly named as issue of this couple. Text says the four castes originated from Brahma's body (Brahmans from face, Kshatrias from breast, Vaisyas from thighs, Sudras from feet), and he is regarded as having mind-born sons (the Prajapatis), but these are not described as born from Sarasvati.

Dasaratha was king of Ayodhya. Rama is Vishnu incarnate. Lakshmana is Rama's faithful brother, described as a partial incarnation of Vishnu. Text also mentions Bharata and Satrughna as Rama's brothers but from a different wife (Kaikeya).

Kaikeya is Dasaratha's youngest wife. Her intrigues caused Rama's banishment. Bharata is described as a partial incarnation of Vishnu. Satrughna is listed as another brother of Rama but the text does not clearly assign him to a specific mother.
Janaka was a king who was childless. Sita was found in a ploughed field (a foundling) and adopted by Janaka. No wife named. Sita identified as Lakshmi incarnate.

source: Part II Ch.V; Table p.4; p.78
comments: Krishna (Vishnu incarnation) was son of Vasudeva and Devaki, but raised by Nanda the shepherd and his wife Yasoda.

Nanda was a shepherd who fostered Krishna. Balarama (Balabhadra) is described as son of Nanda and nominally the elder brother of Krishna. He is the ninth incarnation of Vishnu according to later tradition (replacing Buddha). Text also lists Krishna as raised in this household but his biological parents are Vasudeva and Devaki.

source: Part II Ch.V; Part III Ch.II; pp.76,136-137
comments: Jamadagni was a Rishi. Renuka was beheaded by Parasurama at his father's command for impure thoughts. In Southern Indian tradition Renuka is identified with the Gramadevata Ellammen. Parasurama is Vishnu's sixth incarnation. Five sons total named in the Mahabharata version quoted in the text.

Mohini is Vishnu in female form. Their union produced Ayenar (also called Hari-Hara-putra, i.e. Vishnu-Siva's son), the only male Gramadevata. This is a cosmogonic union between two male gods, one assuming female form.
Puranai is one of Ayenar's two wives. No children mentioned.
Pudkalai is one of Ayenar's two wives. No children mentioned.

Virabhadra was created from Isvara's mouth/wrath to destroy Daksha's sacrifice. Described with 1000 heads and 2000 arms. No mother — produced by divine will alone. Bhadrakali (Rudrakali) accompanied him as emanation of Devi's anger.

source: Part IV Ch.I; Table p.7; pp.178-180
comments: Devendra (Indra) is king of the Devas. Indrani never gave birth herself; Chitraputra (Chitragupta) was born from Indrani's cow as reward for her austerities, but she nursed him. Chitraputra is the recorder deity. Indra's daughter Devayanai married Subhramanya.
Devayanai (Devasena) is described as Indra's martial daughter, promised to Subhramanya. Mother not specified in this text.
Kasyapa is a famous Rishi. Aditi is the mother of the Devas. The present Devendra (Indra) is described as son of Kasyapa. Text says the Devas descended from Kasyapa and Aditi.

Diti is the mother of the Asuras (Daityas/Danavas), the enemies of the Devas. No individual Asura children named as issue of this specific couple in this text. The Vishnu Purana version (cited p.155) says Asuras issued from Brahma's thigh instead.

According to one account in the text, Mahamaya (great illusion) was a giantess created by Isvara's curse. Her union with Kasyapa produced the great Asuras: Surapadma (the tyrant destroyed by Subhramanya), Sinhamukhasura (lion-faced giant), and Tarakasura (elephant-headed giant).

source: Part II Ch.II; Part III Ch.V; pp.47,55,146-151,155
comments: Daksha is one of the Prajapatis (progenitors). Had many daughters. Text mentions Sati (Siva's first wife), and says Aditi and Diti are also his daughters given to Kasyapa. He gave 27 daughters to the Moon. His sacrifice was destroyed by Virabhadra. Mother of daughters not named.
Daksha gave 27 daughters (lunar asterisms) to the Moon. Moon loved Rohini above the rest, was cursed by Daksha with consumption (waxing and waning). No children named.

Pururvasas was a mortal king. Urvasi was the most celebrated celestial courtesan of Svarga. Text names Ayus as the first-born, and says five sons total were born from annual meetings, but only Ayus is named.

parents: Budha (son of Soma) & Tara (wife of Vrihaspati)
comments: Mentioned in the parentage of Pururvasas: he was son of Budha, who was son of Soma (Moon) by Tara (wife of Vrihaspati/Jupiter). Brief genealogical note in the Urvasi legend.

source: Part III Ch.VI; Table p.7; pp.77,154
comments: Ravana was the Rakshasa king of Lanka (Ceylon) with ten heads. Indrajit is his son and conqueror of Indra. Brothers: Kumbhakarna and Vibhishana. Wife not named in this text.

Brief Mahabharata episode mentioned in a note: Ganga assumed human form, married king Santanu, had 8 children. She threw 7 into the Ganges. The 8th, Bhishma, survived. Seven unnamed drowned sons not counted here as they did not survive.

source: Part IV Ch.II; Appendix Ch.II; pp.184-185,202-203
comments: Harischandra was a famous king of great uprightness. His son Rohita was promised as a sacrifice to Varuna but escaped. The Sunahsepha legend is connected to this family.

Ajigarta was a starving Rishi who sold his middle son Sunahsepha to be a sacrifice victim. He was even willing to kill him himself. Sunahsepha was saved by divine intervention and adopted by Visvamitra. Mother present but unnamed.

The five Pandavas (Dharma/Yudhishthira, Bhima, Arjuna, Nakula, Sahadeva) shared one wife Draupadi. No children of this union named in this text. Polyandrous marriage. The text focuses on their war with Duryodhana.

parents: Pradyumna (incarnation of Manmatha) & Mayavati (incarnation of Rati)
comments: Brief mention: Manmatha became incarnate in Pradyumna, firstborn of Krishna. Rati became Mayavati. Their son was Aniruddha. Minimal detail in text.
Jamadagni described as 'son of Richika.' Mother not named in this text.

According to one story in the text, Durga was born with a sheep's head because her parents Kasyapa and Mahamaya cohabited in the form of sheep. This may be the same Mahamaya as in F26 or a different tradition.
